title: Why Pravāh: The Case for Science-Driven River Restoration
excerpt: The idea behind Pravāh, why the work starts with data and field action, and how the two wings fit together.
body:
## The approach

Pravāh is a non-profit river restoration initiative founded in 2026 by engineering students of Indore. The name derives from the Sanskrit word for 'flow' and 'current' —a term used in hydrology, rooted in Indian cultural heritage, and reflective of the organization's dual identity as both a scientific and social movement.

The initiative draws its name from King Bhagirath, who according to Hindu mythology undertook an impossible mission, i.e. bringing the river Ganga to earth through unwavering perseverance. This serves as the founding metaphor: river restoration is a long-term, seemingly impossible mission that is nonetheless worth pursuing with complete commitment. 

The organizational model comprises two wings:

One wing is technical, focused on research and restoration. The other wing is social, focused on people, action, and long-term support.

That structure matters because river work is not only about measurements. It also depends on public trust, clear communication, and steady involvement from people who care.

## What we want to change

The aim is not to make river work look complicated. The aim is to make it useful, consistent, and open enough for more people to help.
